mnemonics: Positive Hessian Holds Up (for convex functions)
Negative Hessian Nods Down (for concave functions)
Detective Hessian Determines Direction (to find minima/maxima)
keyPoints: The Hessian matrix is the square matrix of second-order partial derivatives of a function.
For a twice differentiable function, if the Hessian is positive definite at a point, then that point is a local minimum.
If the Hessian is negative definite at a point, then that point is a local maximum. If it is indefinite, the point is a saddle point.
commonMistakes: Assuming a zero gradient (stationary point) with an indefinite Hessian is an extremum (it's actually a saddle point).
Forgetting to check the definiteness of the Hessian and misinterpreting a saddle point as a minimum or maximum.
